aiTitle: Work on phase 3 in docs plan

content.text: I have a good picture now. Phase 3 is large (the milestone is narrower than the bullet list), and a couple of architectural choices are still open. Let me ask a few questions before designing the slicing.

questions.question: Phase 3's milestone is 'Boot processes each have isolated address spaces; VM handles page faults', but the bullet list under it also names fork() and exec(). fork() in particular requires the PM server (Phase 4). What scope do you want Phase 3 to deliver?

options.label: Milestone-only (Recommended)
options.description: Per-process address spaces, EL1 page-fault handler, VM server with page-fault + mmap + brk. Defer fork()/exec() to Phase 4 alongside PM. Keeps slices PR-sized and the milestone stays the exit criterion.

options.label: Milestone + vm_fork/vm_exec stubs
options.description: Add the VM side of fork/exec (vm_fork copies address space, vm_exec_newmem replaces it) but no PM integration yet — exercised by a kernel-side test driver. Roughly 2 extra slices.

options.label: Full bullet list
options.description: Try to land fork()/exec() end-to-end in Phase 3 even though they depend on Phase 4 work. Requires a minimal in-kernel PM-like driver to invoke SYS_FORK / SYS_EXEC.

options.label: Kernel writes; VM decides (Recommended)
options.description: VM passes (vaddr, phys_frame, prot, asid) to SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_PT_MAP); kernel walks the proc's table (via HHDM) and installs the PTE. Keeps all unsafe paging code in the kernel and avoids VM having to map its own page-table pages.

options.label: VM writes directly (MINIX 3 style)
options.description: VM maps each page-table page into its own address space, writes PTEs, then SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_SETADDRSPACE) installs the new root. Closer to the MINIX 3 reference but adds a sizable 'self-mapping bootstrap' problem (spare-page queue, etc.).

questions.question: Phase 2 was split into 6 PR-sized slices each producing a visible boot trace. How should Phase 3 be sliced?

options.label: Same as Phase 2 — 5–6 PR slices (Recommended)
options.description: Each slice independently builds, boots, and prints observable progress (e.g., '4 procs each on distinct TTBR0', 'page fault routed to VM stub', etc.). Matches the cadence in plan.md and keeps reviews tractable.

options.label: Fewer, larger slices (2–3 PRs)
options.description: Bundle the work into bigger chunks. Faster overall pacing but larger reviews and a bigger blast radius per merge.

questions.question: Two smaller architecture choices that affect the design:

options.label: Use ARMv8 ASIDs in TTBR0_EL1 (Recommended)
options.description: Allocate an 8- or 16-bit ASID per address space so context switch doesn't need a full TLB flush. Adds a small ASID allocator but is a well-trodden design and aligns with how modern ports handle this.

options.label: Use copy-on-write on fork (if fork is in scope)
options.description: Eager copy is simpler to implement first; CoW (refcounted physical pages, write-fault → copy + remap) is what MINIX 3 does for most regions. Only relevant if Phase 3 includes fork.

input.description: Design Phase 3 slice sequence for MINIX 4
input.prompt:
I'm planning Phase 3 of MINIX 4 (Rust microkernel, aarch64-first, MINIX 3 architecture). I need you to validate a proposed 5–6 slice decomposition and surface sequencing problems before I write the final plan.

## Constraints & decisions already made

- **Scope**: Milestone-only — "Boot processes each have isolated address spaces; VM handles page faults." Per-process address spaces, EL1 page-fault handler, VM server with mmap + brk. fork()/exec() deferred to Phase 4 (PM).
- **PTE ownership**: Kernel writes user PTEs via SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_PT_MAP); VM decides what to map. Keeps all unsafe paging in the kernel.
- **Slicing target**: 5–6 PR-sized slices matching Phase 2's cadence — each independently builds, boots in QEMU aarch64-virt, and prints observable progress.
- **ASIDs**: Use ARMv8 ASIDs in TTBR0_EL1[63:48] so context switch doesn't full-flush the TLB.

## Current state (post-Phase 2.6, just merged)

Read these to understand what's already there:
- `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minix-4/docs/plan.md` (slice status, current line ~404–525 has Phase 2.x ship notes; Phase 3 is line 538 onward)
- `/Users/kevinbarnard/src/minix-4/CLAUDE.md` (code conventions — especially the `&mut [Proc; N_PROC_SLOTS]` slice pattern, NLL borrow-capture pattern, RTS↔run-queue helpers)

## Key facts to anchor the design

- EL1 sync vector currently routes everything except SVC to `el0_sync_unexpected` which panics. Translation faults from EL0 (ESR_EL1 EC=0x20 instr abort lower-EL, EC=0x24 data abort lower-EL) need a real handler.

## Proposed slice decomposition (validate / refine)

**3.1 — Physical frame allocator + per-process page tables (kernel-side)**

- Add `Proc::ttbr0_pa: PhysAddr` (and `asid: u16`) field.
- New `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/addrspace.rs`: build an empty L0 + walk-on-demand intermediate tables for a given proc. Rewrite `userland.rs` so stubs A/B/C each get their own freshly-allocated TTBR0; rebuild the boot mappings into the right per-proc tree.
- Context-switch shim: on every EL1→EL0 entry, write `proc.ttbr0_pa | proc.asid << 48` into TTBR0_EL1 and ISB. ASID-tagged TLBI so we don't full-flush.
- IPC user copy: replace `read_volatile(deliver_msg_vir)` with a `walk_pt(proc, vir) -> Option<PhysAddr>` then HHDM access. Validate caller's PT, not "the" PT.
- **Verification**: QEMU boot trace shows distinct TTBR0_EL1 PAs per proc (one-shot debug dump on first schedule); existing ping-pong (stub A↔B) + stub C kernel-call all still pass; deliberately faulting an unmapped VA in stub A doesn't expose stub B's memory.

**3.2 — EL1 page-fault handler + RTS_PAGEFAULT (kernel-resolved)**

- Handler sets caller's `RTS_PAGEFAULT`, records `(addr, flags)` in `Proc::page_fault_state`, dequeues from runqueue.

- Add a 4th EL0 stub D that touches an unmapped page in its heap window then continues; verify trace.
- **Verification**: QEMU trace shows `[pf] proc=D far=0x… resolved → retry`; stub D loops without panic. A/B/C unchanged.

**3.3 — Real SYS_VMCTL + kernel↔VM contract (VM still stubbed in-kernel)**

Add subcall constants in `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`; bump `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE2` (rename to `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3`?) and grow the assert.
- Define `VM_PAGEFAULT` IPC message format + endpoint constant. Page-fault handler now SENDs `VM_PAGEFAULT` to VM endpoint (still no VM process exists — for now a kernel-side "fake VM" handler in the SYSTEM task acts as the receiver, exercising the contract).
- This slice is an end-to-end test of the contract before introducing a real user-space VM.
- **Verification**: Stub D fault now flows kernel pf-handler → SYSTEM "fake-VM" → SYS_VMCTL(PT_MAP) → SYS_VMCTL(CLEAR_PAGEFAULT) → retry. Same trace shape as 3.2 but routed through the new path.

**3.4 — Real VM server boots (mini receive loop)**
- Add `servers/vm/src/main.rs` with sef-style receive loop. For now, only the page-fault path is wired; mmap/brk are deferred.
- Boot image packing: kernel embeds VM ELF in `.boot_image`. New kernel-side ELF loader sufficient for this one binary (text+rodata+data+bss segments only — no dynamic relocs).
- Kernel hand-rolls VM's address space (text/rodata pages, RW data/bss, a stack page) before VM's first SVC. VM's priv slot allows `SYS_VMCTL` and `RECEIVE ANY`.
- Replace the SYSTEM-task fake-VM with real VM (stub D's faults now resolved by the user-space VM process).
- **Verification**: Boot trace shows VM proc starting, receiving its first `VM_PAGEFAULT`, replying. Stub D's faults loop indefinitely with each one round-tripping kernel↔VM↔kernel.

**3.5 — VM region tracking + brk**
- VM keeps a per-process region table (vaddr, length, flags, region kind). Use `BTreeMap<VAddr, Region>` or sorted `Vec` (no heap allocator in VM yet — needs `alloc` crate over a static arena? Pin this down here).
- Implement `VM_BRK` message: extend the heap region. Page faults inside an extended heap region get auto-allocated zero frames.
- Stub D switched to call `VM_BRK` then touch new bytes.
- **Verification**: Stub D's `VM_BRK` grows its heap; subsequent writes succeed without panicking; trace shows region tracking.

**3.6 — VM mmap + Phase-3 polish**
- `VM_MMAP` / `VM_MUNMAP` for anonymous mappings.
- Stub D mmaps a region and writes to it.
- Tidy: SYS_VMCTL subcall coverage assertion, kernel-shared docs, slice notes in plan.md.
- **Milestone reached**: Boot processes each have isolated address spaces (3.1); VM handles page faults (3.4 + 3.5 + 3.6).

## What I want from you

1. **Sequencing risks**: Is there a dependency I'm getting wrong? Anything in 3.1 that actually needs 3.2's pf handler to be in place first, etc.?
2. **Slice 3.1 scope is largest** — should it be split (e.g., frame allocator separate from per-process tables)? Or is its observable milestone (distinct TTBR0s + ping-pong intact) tight enough to keep it one PR?
3. **The "fake VM in SYSTEM task" in 3.3** — clever bridge, or unnecessary complexity? Alternative: skip 3.3 and let 3.4 land both real-SYS_VMCTL and real-VM at once.
4. **VM boot bootstrap**: My plan has the kernel hand-roll VM's initial address space. Is there a cleaner approach using the kernel's frame allocator + map_4k machinery from 3.1?
5. **No-allocator VM**: 3.5 needs per-proc region maps inside VM. Options: (a) static `[ProcSlot; NR_PROCS]` arrays of fixed-size region arrays; (b) bring up a small heap allocator in VM via `linked_list_allocator` or similar; (c) static arena + slab. Which fits the project's "static-tables-first, allocators-only-when-needed" style?
6. **IPC user-copy redesign in 3.1**: Is HHDM-after-PT-walk the right call, or should I temporarily install the target's TTBR0 in the kernel during the copy (uses ARM's `at s1e0r` ATS instruction)?
7. **Anything missing**: Is there a Phase-3 prerequisite I'm not addressing — e.g., kernel-side memcpy with fault catching, exception-safe spinlock for the frame allocator (still single-threaded so probably no), etc.?

Report your assessment in ~600-900 words. Be opinionated. Flag any slice you think is too big or too small; suggest concrete moves between slices. Do not write the final plan file — just give me the analysis.

content.text:
Now I have a solid mental model. I have enough to give a grounded assessment without writing a plan file. Per the system reminder, I should answer comprehensively without making changes, and per the original instructions, I should return the analysis directly as text rather than writing a report file. The instructions explicitly say "Do NOT Write report/summary/findings/analysis .md files. Return findings directly as your final assistant message."

---

## Assessment of the Phase 3 slice decomposition

### 1. Sequencing risks

**Real risk in 3.1:** You list "deliberately faulting an unmapped VA in stub A doesn't expose stub B's memory" as a verification step — but you have **no page-fault handler** in 3.1. Today any EL0 translation fault hits `el0_sync_unexpected` and panics. So either (a) 3.1's verification must be "ping-pong + kernel-call still pass under per-proc TTBR0" with no fault-injection test (the isolation property is structural, not behavioral), or (b) 3.1 has to land a *minimal* "decode EC=0x20/0x24, print FAR/ESR, panic gracefully" stub. I'd go with (b) — it's ~30 lines, gives you a real fault diagnostic instead of the generic "unexpected EL0 sync" trace, and the real `do_page_fault` in 3.2 just replaces the panic body. This also de-risks 3.1 debugging: when something is wrong with your per-proc PT setup, faults are how you'll discover it.

**Real risk in 3.3:** `VM_PAGEFAULT` is delivered by IPC, but per-proc TTBR0s (3.1) plus the new IPC user-copy (HHDM-after-walk) mean the kernel needs to *originate* an IPC message on behalf of a process that is currently blocked on `RTS_PAGEFAULT`. There's no precedent for kernel-originated SEND in the codebase — `mini_send` always runs in the caller's context. You'll need a `mini_notify`-style "kernel sends to VM" path or, more cleanly, the kernel just queues VM's deliver_msg and sets VM runnable. Walk through this in 3.3, not 3.4.

**Order is otherwise correct.** 3.2's RTS_PAGEFAULT is essentially a copy-paste of the slice-2.5 RTS gating pattern; nothing in 3.1 needs it first.

### 2. Slice 3.1 scope — split or not?

**Split it.** As written, 3.1 contains: frame allocator, per-proc page tables, addrspace.rs, context-switch shim, ASIDs, rewriting userland.rs from scratch, and rewriting the IPC user-copy. That's at least three of the prior slices stacked. The IPC user-copy in particular is a behavioral change with its own failure modes (TLB/cache coherence under HHDM access, alignment hazards) entirely orthogonal to per-proc PTs.

Concrete proposal:

- **3.1a — Frame allocator + addrspace plumbing.** Frame allocator over Limine memmap, `mm/` module, `addrspace.rs` with `new_addrspace()` / `map_page()` / `walk_pt()` / `destroy_addrspace()`. Add `Proc::ttbr0_pa` and `Proc::asid` fields but leave them unset. Add a **standalone unit test** path: in `kmain` after `proc::init`, build a throwaway addrspace, map a page, walk it, free it. No EL0 changes. Verification: trace shows "frame alloc OK / map OK / walk OK / free OK".
- **3.1b — Per-proc TTBR0 + context switch + new userland.rs.** Now `userland_bootstrap` uses the 3.1a machinery instead of the static `L0/L1/L2/L3` arrays. Context-switch shim writes TTBR0_EL1 with ASID-tagged value. IPC user-copy keeps using `read_volatile` because in this slice the kernel just installs the caller's TTBR0 before the read (you're already at EL1 with the caller's TTBR0 live anyway, since `el1_svc_tail` runs in caller context). Verification: distinct TTBR0_EL1 PAs per proc; ping-pong + stub C kernel-call unchanged.
- **3.1c (folds into 3.2 or 3.3) — HHDM-walking user-copy.** Defer the user-copy redesign until VM exists. The current `read_volatile` works fine while the SVC handler runs in caller context. You only *need* HHDM-walking when the kernel reads/writes another process's memory (e.g., to deliver an IPC message *on behalf of* a blocked sender into the receiver's deliver_msg_vir — that crosses address spaces). That's a 3.3+ concern.

This is the single biggest sequencing improvement you can make.

### 3. The "fake VM in SYSTEM task" in 3.3

**Drop it.** It's clever but it costs you two API designs you'll throw away (a kernel-internal fake-VM dispatcher and a way to route `VM_PAGEFAULT` to a kernel target), and it inverts the trust direction (the SYSTEM task is the kernel-call *receiver*, not an *originator* — making it send IPC back to the page-fault handler is structurally weird).

Better split: keep 3.3 as "real SYS_VMCTL subcalls + define `VM_PAGEFAULT` wire format" but exercise SYS_VMCTL from **stub C** (or a new stub D), not from a fake VM. Stub C already has `k_call_mask` plumbing; give it temporary `SYS_VMCTL` access and have it issue `VMCTL_PT_MAP` for an address it owns, then touch it. That tests the SYS_VMCTL surface end-to-end with no fake-VM scaffolding. Then 3.4 lands real VM and 3.4's verification is "VM resolves a real page fault" — a sharper milestone than "VM replaces fake VM."

This also collapses your concern about the kernel-originated `VM_PAGEFAULT` SEND: you only design it once, with a real receiver, in 3.4.

### 4. VM boot bootstrap

The kernel hand-rolling VM's address space is unavoidable (chicken-and-egg) but **should reuse the 3.1a machinery**, not duplicate it. Concretely: in 3.4's `vm_bootstrap`, call `addrspace::new()`, then walk the embedded VM ELF's PHDRs and call `addrspace::map_page()` for each page in each LOAD segment, allocating frames out of your frame allocator and copying via HHDM. Same code path VM itself will use for stubs later. The only "hand-rolled" part is the ELF loader — and you said in 3.4 you'll build a minimal one.

One thing missing from your sketch: VM needs to know **which frames are already taken** by its own image. Easiest: have the frame allocator track the VM image's frames as reserved from boot (same way it reserves the kernel image's frames). Otherwise VM's later mmaps could collide with its own .text.

### 5. No-allocator VM

Go with **(a) static `[ProcSlot; NR_PROCS]` arrays of fixed-size region arrays** for 3.5, and defer (b) until you genuinely need variable-size structures. Rationale:

- Project style is overwhelmingly "static-tables-first" (PROC_TABLE, PRIV_TABLE, run queues, IMAGE). Bringing in `linked_list_allocator` is a big philosophical shift to fund a single feature.
- A `[Region; 16]` per proc handles text/data/bss/heap/stack + ~10 mmaps. If you need more, increase the constant. Real MINIX 3 VM uses `region_init`/`region_extend` on chained dynamically-allocated nodes — but you don't need that fidelity for the Phase 3 milestone.
- This punts the heap-allocator decision to the slice that actually demands it (likely PM in Phase 4, where the `mproc` table can't reasonably be fixed-size if you want any-time fork).

(b) is the right answer eventually; (c) static arena + slab is the worst of both worlds.

### 6. IPC user-copy: HHDM-walk vs `at s1e0r`

**HHDM-walk after `walk_pt(proc, vir)`.** Reasons:

- `AT S1E0R` writes its result into `PAR_EL1`, which is a global system register. Concurrent access from any nested fault path corrupts it. You're single-CPU now, but the discipline is wrong.
- `AT S1E0R` only checks "would EL0 be able to access this VA *in the current TTBR0*". To use it cross-process you'd still have to swap TTBR0 first — at which point you've paid the TLBI cost and might as well just do the load.
- HHDM-walk is what every other modern microkernel (seL4, Helios) does, and it composes cleanly with your existing `kernel_va_to_pa` / HHDM offset infrastructure.
- It also gives you a natural place to surface "VA is unmapped" as a clean `Err(EFAULT)` instead of as a fault that needs trampoline-based recovery.

### 7. Anything missing

A few prerequisites you haven't called out:

1. **TTBR1 invariant on context switch.** Limine owns TTBR1_EL1 and your code never touches it. The slice-2.3 `activate_user_ttbr0` deliberately only flips bit 7 of TCR. Document and *assert* in the 3.1b context-switch path that TTBR1_EL1 is never written — easy to slip when adding TLBI ops.

2. **ASID allocation policy.** ARMv8 supports 8 or 16-bit ASIDs (TCR_EL1.AS). With NR_PROCS=1024 and 8-bit ASIDs you wrap. Either (a) program `TCR_EL1.AS=1` for 16-bit (then assert Limine left it 0 and you're the one changing it — touchy), or (b) implement ASID-rollover with a full TLBI when you wrap. For Phase 3 with <20 procs, just use 8-bit ASIDs and panic on overflow; promote to a real rollover allocator in Phase 4 when fork() actually creates ASIDs at speed.

3. **Frame allocator concurrency.** Single-CPU and IRQ-masked at the alloc sites is fine, but the page-fault handler runs with IRQs *possibly* re-enabled (your slice 2.4 SPSR has I=0). If the page-fault handler calls into the frame allocator, you need to mask IRQs around the alloc, or document that page-fault allocs run with IRQs masked. Cheaper: page-fault flow is "RTS_PAGEFAULT → enqueue VM → return to scheduler → VM runs and allocates" — the allocator never runs in fault context. Make this explicit in the 3.2 plan.

4. **`NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE2` rename.** Don't rename — add `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3 = 14 + N_new_calls` and update the assert to `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3`. Keeping the old constant around for one slice avoids cross-cutting churn.

5. **The "boot procs already in slots 0..10" assumption** will need to grow once VM occupies a slot. You probably want a `VM_PROC_NR` constant added in 3.4, not invented mid-implementation. Add it to `kernel-shared/src/com.rs` alongside the existing `SYSTEM`, `RS_PROC_NR`, etc.

6. **No `userland.rs` cleanup plan.** After 3.4, stubs A/B/C are vestigial — they still test ping-pong but they're competing for cognitive space with the real VM-managed proc D. Slice 3.6's "polish" should include deleting A/B/C, or explicitly preserving them as regression coverage. Make the call now.

### Suggested re-sliced sequence

1. **3.1a** Frame allocator + addrspace API (no EL0 changes)
2. **3.1b** Per-proc TTBR0s + ASIDs + new userland.rs + minimal pf-diagnostic handler (EC=0x20/0x24 → print and panic, replacing the generic `el0_sync_unexpected`)
3. **3.2** Real `do_page_fault` + RTS_PAGEFAULT + kernel-resolved "heap window" fault retry + stub D
4. **3.3** Real SYS_VMCTL subcalls; stub D exercises SYS_VMCTL directly (no fake VM)
5. **3.4** Real VM server boots, ELF loader, kernel↔VM page-fault protocol, HHDM-walking IPC delivery for cross-AS messages
6. **3.5** VM region tracking + brk
7. **3.6** mmap + cleanup (delete or retain stubs A/B/C, finalize SYS_VMCTL subcall coverage, plan.md notes)

That's 7 slices, not 6. The extra slice buys you: a clean frame-allocator-only PR (easy to review, easy to revert), a defensible 3.1b that doesn't depend on a missing pf handler, and a 3.3 with a sharper unit-test story. If you must keep 6, fold 3.5+3.6 together — brk and mmap share enough VM scaffolding to be one PR.

### Critical files for implementation

- /Users/kevinbarnard/src/minix-4/kernel/src/arch/aarch64/mmu.rs
- /Users/kevinbarnard/src/minix-4/kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs
- /Users/kevinbarnard/src/minix-4/kernel/src/ipc/message.rs
- /Users/kevinbarnard/src/minix-4/kernel/src/system/mod.rs
- /Users/kevinbarnard/src/minix-4/kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs
- /Users/kevinbarnard/src/minix-4/kernel/src/proc/proc_struct.rs

- Add EC=0x20/0x24 dispatch in `el0_64_sync_entry` → `do_page_fault(esr, far, elr)`.
- Handler sets caller's `RTS_PAGEFAULT`, records `(addr, flags)` in `Proc::page_fault_state`, dequeues from runqueue.
- For this slice the kernel resolves itself: if `addr` is in a pre-declared "heap window" for the faulting proc, allocate a frame, map RW+NX, clear RTS, requeue. Otherwise hand off to `el0_sync_unexpected` for now (Phase-3 SIGSEGV is later).
- Add a 4th EL0 stub D that touches an unmapped page in its heap window then continues; verify trace.
- **Verification**: QEMU trace shows `[pf] proc=D far=0x… resolved → retry`; stub D loops without panic. A/B/C unchanged.

**3.3 — Real SYS_VMCTL + kernel↔VM contract (VM still stubbed in-kernel)**
- Replace `stubs::do_vmctl` with a real dispatcher over a small set of subcalls: `VMCTL_PT_MAP`, `VMCTL_PT_UNMAP`, `VMCTL_CLEAR_PAGEFAULT`, `VMCTL_VMINHIBIT_SET/CLEAR`, `VMCTL_GET_PAGEFAULT` (returns most recent pf state to VM). Add subcall constants in `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`; bump `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE2` (rename to `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3`?) and grow the assert.
- Define `VM_PAGEFAULT` IPC message format + endpoint constant. Page-fault handler now SENDs `VM_PAGEFAULT` to VM endpoint (still no VM process exists — for now a kernel-side "fake VM" handler in the SYSTEM task acts as the receiver, exercising the contract).
- This slice is an end-to-end test of the contract before introducing a real user-space VM.
- **Verification**: Stub D fault now flows kernel pf-handler → SYSTEM "fake-VM" → SYS_VMCTL(PT_MAP) → SYS_VMCTL(CLEAR_PAGEFAULT) → retry. Same trace shape as 3.2 but routed through the new path.

**3.4 — Real VM server boots (mini receive loop)**
- Add `servers/vm/src/main.rs` with sef-style receive loop. For now, only the page-fault path is wired; mmap/brk are deferred.
- Boot image packing: kernel embeds VM ELF in `.boot_image`. New kernel-side ELF loader sufficient for this one binary (text+rodata+data+bss segments only — no dynamic relocs).
- Kernel hand-rolls VM's address space (text/rodata pages, RW data/bss, a stack page) before VM's first SVC. VM's priv slot allows `SYS_VMCTL` and `RECEIVE ANY`.
- Replace the SYSTEM-task fake-VM with real VM (stub D's faults now resolved by the user-space VM process).
- **Verification**: Boot trace shows VM proc starting, receiving its first `VM_PAGEFAULT`, replying. Stub D's faults loop indefinitely with each one round-tripping kernel↔VM↔kernel.

**3.5 — VM region tracking + brk**
- VM keeps a per-process region table (vaddr, length, flags, region kind). Use `BTreeMap<VAddr, Region>` or sorted `Vec` (no heap allocator in VM yet — needs `alloc` crate over a static arena? Pin this down here).
- Implement `VM_BRK` message: extend the heap region. Page faults inside an extended heap region get auto-allocated zero frames.
- Stub D switched to call `VM_BRK` then touch new bytes.
- **Verification**: Stub D's `VM_BRK` grows its heap; subsequent writes succeed without panicking; trace shows region tracking.

**3.6 — VM mmap + Phase-3 polish**
- `VM_MMAP` / `VM_MUNMAP` for anonymous mappings.
- Stub D mmaps a region and writes to it.
- Tidy: SYS_VMCTL subcall coverage assertion, kernel-shared docs, slice notes in plan.md.
- **Milestone reached**: Boot processes each have isolated address spaces (3.1); VM handles page faults (3.4 + 3.5 + 3.6).

## What I want from you

1. **Sequencing risks**: Is there a dependency I'm getting wrong? Anything in 3.1 that actually needs 3.2's pf handler to be in place first, etc.?
2. **Slice 3.1 scope is largest** — should it be split (e.g., frame allocator separate from per-process tables)? Or is its observable milestone (distinct TTBR0s + ping-pong intact) tight enough to keep it one PR?
3. **The "fake VM in SYSTEM task" in 3.3** — clever bridge, or unnecessary complexity? Alternative: skip 3.3 and let 3.4 land both real-SYS_VMCTL and real-VM at once.
4. **VM boot bootstrap**: My plan has the kernel hand-roll VM's initial address space. Is there a cleaner approach using the kernel's frame allocator + map_4k machinery from 3.1?
5. **No-allocator VM**: 3.5 needs per-proc region maps inside VM. Options: (a) static `[ProcSlot; NR_PROCS]` arrays of fixed-size region arrays; (b) bring up a small heap allocator in VM via `linked_list_allocator` or similar; (c) static arena + slab. Which fits the project's "static-tables-first, allocators-only-when-needed" style?
6. **IPC user-copy redesign in 3.1**: Is HHDM-after-PT-walk the right call, or should I temporarily install the target's TTBR0 in the kernel during the copy (uses ARM's `at s1e0r` ATS instruction)?
7. **Anything missing**: Is there a Phase-3 prerequisite I'm not addressing — e.g., kernel-side memcpy with fault catching, exception-safe spinlock for the frame allocator (still single-threaded so probably no), etc.?

Report your assessment in ~600-900 words. Be opinionated. Flag any slice you think is too big or too small; suggest concrete moves between slices. Do not write the final plan file — just give me the analysis.

Now I have a solid mental model. I have enough to give a grounded assessment without writing a plan file. Per the system reminder, I should answer comprehensively without making changes, and per the original instructions, I should return the analysis directly as text rather than writing a report file. The instructions explicitly say "Do NOT Write report/summary/findings/analysis .md files. Return findings directly as your final assistant message."

---

## Assessment of the Phase 3 slice decomposition

### 1. Sequencing risks

**Real risk in 3.1:** You list "deliberately faulting an unmapped VA in stub A doesn't expose stub B's memory" as a verification step — but you have **no page-fault handler** in 3.1. Today any EL0 translation fault hits `el0_sync_unexpected` and panics. So either (a) 3.1's verification must be "ping-pong + kernel-call still pass under per-proc TTBR0" with no fault-injection test (the isolation property is structural, not behavioral), or (b) 3.1 has to land a *minimal* "decode EC=0x20/0x24, print FAR/ESR, panic gracefully" stub. I'd go with (b) — it's ~30 lines, gives you a real fault diagnostic instead of the generic "unexpected EL0 sync" trace, and the real `do_page_fault` in 3.2 just replaces the panic body. This also de-risks 3.1 debugging: when something is wrong with your per-proc PT setup, faults are how you'll discover it.

**Real risk in 3.3:** `VM_PAGEFAULT` is delivered by IPC, but per-proc TTBR0s (3.1) plus the new IPC user-copy (HHDM-after-walk) mean the kernel needs to *originate* an IPC message on behalf of a process that is currently blocked on `RTS_PAGEFAULT`. There's no precedent for kernel-originated SEND in the codebase — `mini_send` always runs in the caller's context. You'll need a `mini_notify`-style "kernel sends to VM" path or, more cleanly, the kernel just queues VM's deliver_msg and sets VM runnable. Walk through this in 3.3, not 3.4.

**Order is otherwise correct.** 3.2's RTS_PAGEFAULT is essentially a copy-paste of the slice-2.5 RTS gating pattern; nothing in 3.1 needs it first.

### 2. Slice 3.1 scope — split or not?

**Split it.** As written, 3.1 contains: frame allocator, per-proc page tables, addrspace.rs, context-switch shim, ASIDs, rewriting userland.rs from scratch, and rewriting the IPC user-copy. That's at least three of the prior slices stacked. The IPC user-copy in particular is a behavioral change with its own failure modes (TLB/cache coherence under HHDM access, alignment hazards) entirely orthogonal to per-proc PTs.

Concrete proposal:

- **3.1a — Frame allocator + addrspace plumbing.** Frame allocator over Limine memmap, `mm/` module, `addrspace.rs` with `new_addrspace()` / `map_page()` / `walk_pt()` / `destroy_addrspace()`. Add `Proc::ttbr0_pa` and `Proc::asid` fields but leave them unset. Add a **standalone unit test** path: in `kmain` after `proc::init`, build a throwaway addrspace, map a page, walk it, free it. No EL0 changes. Verification: trace shows "frame alloc OK / map OK / walk OK / free OK".
- **3.1b — Per-proc TTBR0 + context switch + new userland.rs.** Now `userland_bootstrap` uses the 3.1a machinery instead of the static `L0/L1/L2/L3` arrays. Context-switch shim writes TTBR0_EL1 with ASID-tagged value. IPC user-copy keeps using `read_volatile` because in this slice the kernel just installs the caller's TTBR0 before the read (you're already at EL1 with the caller's TTBR0 live anyway, since `el1_svc_tail` runs in caller context). Verification: distinct TTBR0_EL1 PAs per proc; ping-pong + stub C kernel-call unchanged.
- **3.1c (folds into 3.2 or 3.3) — HHDM-walking user-copy.** Defer the user-copy redesign until VM exists. The current `read_volatile` works fine while the SVC handler runs in caller context. You only *need* HHDM-walking when the kernel reads/writes another process's memory (e.g., to deliver an IPC message *on behalf of* a blocked sender into the receiver's deliver_msg_vir — that crosses address spaces). That's a 3.3+ concern.

This is the single biggest sequencing improvement you can make.

### 3. The "fake VM in SYSTEM task" in 3.3

**Drop it.** It's clever but it costs you two API designs you'll throw away (a kernel-internal fake-VM dispatcher and a way to route `VM_PAGEFAULT` to a kernel target), and it inverts the trust direction (the SYSTEM task is the kernel-call *receiver*, not an *originator* — making it send IPC back to the page-fault handler is structurally weird).

Better split: keep 3.3 as "real SYS_VMCTL subcalls + define `VM_PAGEFAULT` wire format" but exercise SYS_VMCTL from **stub C** (or a new stub D), not from a fake VM. Stub C already has `k_call_mask` plumbing; give it temporary `SYS_VMCTL` access and have it issue `VMCTL_PT_MAP` for an address it owns, then touch it. That tests the SYS_VMCTL surface end-to-end with no fake-VM scaffolding. Then 3.4 lands real VM and 3.4's verification is "VM resolves a real page fault" — a sharper milestone than "VM replaces fake VM."

This also collapses your concern about the kernel-originated `VM_PAGEFAULT` SEND: you only design it once, with a real receiver, in 3.4.

### 4. VM boot bootstrap

The kernel hand-rolling VM's address space is unavoidable (chicken-and-egg) but **should reuse the 3.1a machinery**, not duplicate it. Concretely: in 3.4's `vm_bootstrap`, call `addrspace::new()`, then walk the embedded VM ELF's PHDRs and call `addrspace::map_page()` for each page in each LOAD segment, allocating frames out of your frame allocator and copying via HHDM. Same code path VM itself will use for stubs later. The only "hand-rolled" part is the ELF loader — and you said in 3.4 you'll build a minimal one.

One thing missing from your sketch: VM needs to know **which frames are already taken** by its own image. Easiest: have the frame allocator track the VM image's frames as reserved from boot (same way it reserves the kernel image's frames). Otherwise VM's later mmaps could collide with its own .text.

### 5. No-allocator VM

Go with **(a) static `[ProcSlot; NR_PROCS]` arrays of fixed-size region arrays** for 3.5, and defer (b) until you genuinely need variable-size structures. Rationale:

- Project style is overwhelmingly "static-tables-first" (PROC_TABLE, PRIV_TABLE, run queues, IMAGE). Bringing in `linked_list_allocator` is a big philosophical shift to fund a single feature.
- A `[Region; 16]` per proc handles text/data/bss/heap/stack + ~10 mmaps. If you need more, increase the constant. Real MINIX 3 VM uses `region_init`/`region_extend` on chained dynamically-allocated nodes — but you don't need that fidelity for the Phase 3 milestone.
- This punts the heap-allocator decision to the slice that actually demands it (likely PM in Phase 4, where the `mproc` table can't reasonably be fixed-size if you want any-time fork).

(b) is the right answer eventually; (c) static arena + slab is the worst of both worlds.

### 6. IPC user-copy: HHDM-walk vs `at s1e0r`

**HHDM-walk after `walk_pt(proc, vir)`.** Reasons:

- `AT S1E0R` writes its result into `PAR_EL1`, which is a global system register. Concurrent access from any nested fault path corrupts it. You're single-CPU now, but the discipline is wrong.
- `AT S1E0R` only checks "would EL0 be able to access this VA *in the current TTBR0*". To use it cross-process you'd still have to swap TTBR0 first — at which point you've paid the TLBI cost and might as well just do the load.
- HHDM-walk is what every other modern microkernel (seL4, Helios) does, and it composes cleanly with your existing `kernel_va_to_pa` / HHDM offset infrastructure.
- It also gives you a natural place to surface "VA is unmapped" as a clean `Err(EFAULT)` instead of as a fault that needs trampoline-based recovery.

### 7. Anything missing

A few prerequisites you haven't called out:

1. **TTBR1 invariant on context switch.** Limine owns TTBR1_EL1 and your code never touches it. The slice-2.3 `activate_user_ttbr0` deliberately only flips bit 7 of TCR. Document and *assert* in the 3.1b context-switch path that TTBR1_EL1 is never written — easy to slip when adding TLBI ops.

2. **ASID allocation policy.** ARMv8 supports 8 or 16-bit ASIDs (TCR_EL1.AS). With NR_PROCS=1024 and 8-bit ASIDs you wrap. Either (a) program `TCR_EL1.AS=1` for 16-bit (then assert Limine left it 0 and you're the one changing it — touchy), or (b) implement ASID-rollover with a full TLBI when you wrap. For Phase 3 with <20 procs, just use 8-bit ASIDs and panic on overflow; promote to a real rollover allocator in Phase 4 when fork() actually creates ASIDs at speed.

3. **Frame allocator concurrency.** Single-CPU and IRQ-masked at the alloc sites is fine, but the page-fault handler runs with IRQs *possibly* re-enabled (your slice 2.4 SPSR has I=0). If the page-fault handler calls into the frame allocator, you need to mask IRQs around the alloc, or document that page-fault allocs run with IRQs masked. Cheaper: page-fault flow is "RTS_PAGEFAULT → enqueue VM → return to scheduler → VM runs and allocates" — the allocator never runs in fault context. Make this explicit in the 3.2 plan.

4. **`NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE2` rename.** Don't rename — add `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3 = 14 + N_new_calls` and update the assert to `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3`. Keeping the old constant around for one slice avoids cross-cutting churn.

5. **The "boot procs already in slots 0..10" assumption** will need to grow once VM occupies a slot. You probably want a `VM_PROC_NR` constant added in 3.4, not invented mid-implementation. Add it to `kernel-shared/src/com.rs` alongside the existing `SYSTEM`, `RS_PROC_NR`, etc.

6. **No `userland.rs` cleanup plan.** After 3.4, stubs A/B/C are vestigial — they still test ping-pong but they're competing for cognitive space with the real VM-managed proc D. Slice 3.6's "polish" should include deleting A/B/C, or explicitly preserving them as regression coverage. Make the call now.

### Suggested re-sliced sequence

1. **3.1a** Frame allocator + addrspace API (no EL0 changes)
2. **3.1b** Per-proc TTBR0s + ASIDs + new userland.rs + minimal pf-diagnostic handler (EC=0x20/0x24 → print and panic, replacing the generic `el0_sync_unexpected`)
3. **3.2** Real `do_page_fault` + RTS_PAGEFAULT + kernel-resolved "heap window" fault retry + stub D
4. **3.3** Real SYS_VMCTL subcalls; stub D exercises SYS_VMCTL directly (no fake VM)
5. **3.4** Real VM server boots, ELF loader, kernel↔VM page-fault protocol, HHDM-walking IPC delivery for cross-AS messages
6. **3.5** VM region tracking + brk
7. **3.6** mmap + cleanup (delete or retain stubs A/B/C, finalize SYS_VMCTL subcall coverage, plan.md notes)

That's 7 slices, not 6. The extra slice buys you: a clean frame-allocator-only PR (easy to review, easy to revert), a defensible 3.1b that doesn't depend on a missing pf handler, and a 3.3 with a sharper unit-test story. If you must keep 6, fold 3.5+3.6 together — brk and mmap share enough VM scaffolding to be one PR.

### Critical files for implementation

- /Users/kevinbarnard/src/minix-4/kernel/src/arch/aarch64/mmu.rs
- /Users/kevinbarnard/src/minix-4/kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs
- /Users/kevinbarnard/src/minix-4/kernel/src/ipc/message.rs
- /Users/kevinbarnard/src/minix-4/kernel/src/system/mod.rs
- /Users/kevinbarnard/src/minix-4/kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs
- /Users/kevinbarnard/src/minix-4/kernel/src/proc/proc_struct.rs

input.content:
# Phase 3: VM Server + Memory Management

## Context

Phase 2 (slices 2.1–2.6, all shipped) gave MINIX 4 a working microkernel: IPC primitives, GICv3+timer scheduling, SVC kernel-call dispatch with a real `SYS_GETINFO`. Today every EL0 process (stubs A, B, C) shares a **single TTBR0**, all page tables are static `.bss` arrays, there is **no physical frame allocator**, and any EL0 translation fault hits `el0_sync_unexpected` and panics. The `servers/vm` crate is an empty stub; `SYS_VMCTL` is one of the 13 ENOSYS placeholders from slice 2.6.

Phase 3 delivers the milestone **"Boot processes each have isolated address spaces; VM handles page faults"** — per-process page tables with ARMv8 ASIDs, an EL1 page-fault handler, a real user-space VM server with brk + mmap, and the SYS_VMCTL contract that lets VM mediate paging while the kernel retains all unsafe PTE writes. fork()/exec() are deferred to Phase 4 (they require the PM server).

**Architecture choices:**
- **PTE ownership**: Kernel writes user PTEs. VM passes `(vaddr, phys_frame, prot, asid)` to `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_PT_MAP)`; the kernel walks the target proc's page table via HHDM and installs the PTE. Keeps all unsafe paging code in the kernel and avoids VM's self-mapping bootstrap problem.
- **ASIDs**: 8-bit ASIDs in `TTBR0_EL1[55:48]` (TCR_EL1.AS left at 0, the Limine default). Allocator panics on overflow; real rollover deferred to Phase 4 when fork() actually churns ASIDs.
- **Page-table ownership split**: Kernel owns TTBR1 (untouched from Limine). Each proc gets its own TTBR0 root; context switch updates TTBR0_EL1 with ASID-tagged value and ISBs. TLBI is ASID-tagged, never full-flush.
- **No allocator in VM**: Region tables are static `[Region; N]` arrays per proc inside VM. `linked_list_allocator` deferred until Phase 4 where PM needs it.
- **Kernel reads user memory via HHDM after walking the target proc's page table**, not via `AT S1E0R`. Composes with existing `kernel_va_to_pa` infra and surfaces "VA unmapped" as a clean `Err(EFAULT)`.
- **Stubs A/B/C from Phase 2.5/2.6 stay** as regression coverage; they get migrated to per-proc TTBR0s in 3.1b and run through the rest of Phase 3.

---

## Slice plan (7 PR-sized slices)

Each slice independently builds, boots in QEMU `aarch64-unknown-none`, and prints observable progress. Same cadence as Phase 2. Update `docs/plan.md` slice-status markers (`◀ next` / `◀ ready` / `✓ shipped`) as part of each PR.

### Slice 3.1a — Physical frame allocator + addrspace API (kernel-only, no EL0 changes)

- New `kernel/src/mm/mod.rs` + `mm/frame.rs`: bitmap frame allocator over Limine memmap usable regions. Reserve frames covering: kernel image (`_kern_start` … `_kern_end`), the embedded `.boot_image` section, the existing static stub code/stack pages from Phase 2.5/2.6 (mark all as in-use at boot so they aren't double-allocated). `alloc_frame() -> Option<PhysFrame>`, `free_frame(PhysFrame)`, `reserved_range(start, end)`.
- New `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/addrspace.rs`: `AddrSpace::new() -> Result<AddrSpace>` allocates an L0 frame and zeros it via HHDM. `map_page(va, pa, prot)` walks/allocates intermediate L1/L2/L3 tables on demand using `alloc_frame()`, writes the leaf PTE. `walk_pt(va) -> Option<PhysAddr>` for kernel-side reads later. `destroy()` walks all live mappings, frees intermediate tables (free leaf frames is the caller's job — leaves room for shared/CoW pages later).
- `kmain` smoke test: after `proc::init`, build a throwaway addrspace, map 4 distinct pages, walk each, destroy it. Trace `[mm] frame_alloc OK / map OK / walk OK / free OK`.
- No `Proc` field changes yet, no userland changes, no IPC changes.
- **Files**: new `kernel/src/mm/{mod,frame}.rs`, new `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/addrspace.rs`, additions to `kernel/src/main.rs` (smoke test gated on a `cfg(feature = "mm_smoke")` or just inlined for the slice and removed in 3.1b).
- **Verification**: QEMU trace shows the smoke-test sequence and clean boot; A/B/C ping-pong + stub C kernel-call unchanged.

### Slice 3.1b — Per-process TTBR0s + ASIDs + minimal pf-diagnostic handler

- Add `Proc::ttbr0_pa: PhysAddr` and `Proc::asid: u8`. Add ASID allocator (8-bit, panic on overflow, single-threaded boot invariant documented).
- Rewrite `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs`: each stub gets its own `AddrSpace`, its own freshly allocated code+stack frames, freshly mapped. Static `L0_TABLE`/`L1_TABLE`/`L2_TABLE`/`L3_CODE_TABLE`/`L3_STACK_TABLE` arrays are removed.
- Context switch (`el1_return_to_user` tail in `trap.S` / `sched::schedule_next` in `proc/sched.rs`): before ERET, write `(ttbr0_pa | (asid as u64) << 48)` into `TTBR0_EL1`, ISB. ASID-tagged TLBI on first switch into a freshly created addrspace. Document and assert that `TTBR1_EL1` is never written (Limine owns it).
- Replace `el0_sync_unexpected` panic for EC=0x20 (instr abort lower EL) and EC=0x24 (data abort lower EL) with a one-page `do_page_fault_stub` that decodes ESR_EL1/FAR_EL1/ELR_EL1 + the faulting proc's name and panics with a clean dump. The real handler lands in 3.2; this slice just trades a generic panic for a useful diagnostic so 3.1b debugging is tractable.
- IPC user-copy in `ipc/message.rs` keeps `read_volatile(deliver_msg_vir)` unchanged — the SVC handler runs in caller context with caller's TTBR0 live, so single-AS reads still work. Cross-AS redesign lands in 3.4 when VM exists.
- **Files**: `kernel/src/proc/proc_struct.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs` (rewrite), `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/trap.S`, new `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/asid.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs`.
- **Verification**: One-shot debug dump at first schedule shows distinct `TTBR0_EL1`/ASID per proc (A=asid1, B=asid2, C=asid3). Existing ping-pong + SYS_GETINFO traces unchanged. Inject a deliberate unmapped EL0 access (temporary debug stub) and see the new diagnostic instead of the generic `el0_sync_unexpected`; remove the deliberate fault before merging.

### Slice 3.2 — Real page-fault handler + RTS_PAGEFAULT (kernel-resolved)

- Promote the 3.1b diagnostic into `do_page_fault(esr, far, elr)`: classify (translation vs permission), set caller's `RTS_PAGEFAULT`, record `(addr, flags, ip)` in a new `Proc::page_fault_state` field, dequeue from runqueue using the slice-2.5 `rts_set` helper (capture-then-borrow-end NLL pattern), call `reschedule`.
- For this slice, the kernel resolves itself: each proc has a "heap window" range stored in `Proc` (set by userland.rs at boot — e.g., `[0x0100_0000, 0x0100_4000)`). If `far` falls in that window, kernel allocates a frame via 3.1a's allocator, maps it RW+NX via the proc's addrspace, ASID-tagged TLBI, clears `RTS_PAGEFAULT` via `rts_unset`, requeues. Otherwise hand off to the panic path (real SIGSEGV is Phase 4 PM work).
- Page-fault flow runs **outside** allocator-from-fault-context concern: the handler enqueues and returns to the scheduler before any allocation; allocation happens when the proc (or VM in later slices) is next scheduled. Document this invariant.
- Add a 4th EL0 stub D (ProcNr 14, Priv slot 19, code at 0x40_0000-aligned VA distinct from A/B/C, heap window 0x0100_0000+): touches an unmapped page in its heap window, loops. Stub D shares one IMAGE entry like C — minimal extra `install_stub_d_priv` helper.
- **Files**: `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs`, new `kernel/src/proc/page_fault.rs`, `kernel/src/proc/proc_struct.rs` (add `page_fault_state` + `heap_window`), `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs` (stub D + heap window setup), `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/user_stub.S` (stub D blob), new RTS flag in `kernel/src/proc/flags.rs`.
- **Verification**: QEMU trace `[pf] proc=D far=0x01000000 → alloc frame=0xXXX, map, retry`; stub D loops without panic. A/B/C unchanged.

### Slice 3.3 — Real SYS_VMCTL subcalls, exercised from a stub (no VM yet)

- New `kernel/src/system/do_vmctl.rs` replacing the `stubs::do_vmctl` ENOSYS placeholder. Subcalls:
  - `VMCTL_PT_MAP(endpoint, vaddr, pa, prot)` — install one PTE in target proc's AS via the 3.1a `map_page` path. Returns OK or EINVAL/ENOMEM.
  - `VMCTL_PT_UNMAP(endpoint, vaddr)` — clear one PTE + ASID-tagged TLBI.
  - `VMCTL_CLEAR_PAGEFAULT(endpoint)` — `rts_unset(RTS_PAGEFAULT)` on target, requeue.
  - `VMCTL_GET_PAGEFAULT(endpoint, &out)` — return `page_fault_state` to caller (only valid while target has `RTS_PAGEFAULT` set).
  - `VMCTL_VMINHIBIT_SET / _CLEAR(endpoint)` — block scheduling of target while VM mutates its AS.
- `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`: add `VMCTL_*` subcall constants. Add a new `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3 = 14` (calls list unchanged, just renamed for the next slice's const-assert; keep `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE2` as an alias for one slice to minimize churn — drop in 3.4). The compile-time `assert!` in `system/mod.rs` updates to the new name.
- Switch the 3.2 kernel-resolved heap-window into stub D *itself* calling `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_PT_MAP)` to install heap pages it pre-faulted on. This exercises the full SYS_VMCTL surface end-to-end without inventing a "fake VM in SYSTEM". Stub D's priv slot gains `SYS_VMCTL` in its `k_call_mask`.
- The kernel-resolved fast path from 3.2 stays in place but is now reachable only from procs *without* `SYS_VMCTL` access — keeps the slice composable.
- **Files**: new `kernel/src/system/do_vmctl.rs`, `kernel/src/system/mod.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/user_stub.S` (stub D rewritten to issue SYS_VMCTL), `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs` (stub D priv update).
- **Verification**: Stub D fault → kernel pf handler sets RTS_PAGEFAULT → stub D somehow needs to learn it faulted... actually since stub D *can't* run while blocked on RTS_PAGEFAULT, the test pattern in this slice is **pre-emptive**: stub D first calls `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_PT_MAP)` to populate its heap, then accesses it. The on-demand fault → VMCTL_GET_PAGEFAULT path is exercised by VM in 3.4. Trace shows `[ksys VMCTL_PT_MAP] proc=D va=0x01000000 pa=0xXXX result=0` then successful access.

### Slice 3.4 — Real VM server boots, ELF loader, cross-AS IPC delivery

- `servers/vm/src/main.rs`: minimal sef-style receive loop. Handles `VM_PAGEFAULT` only (mmap/brk in 3.5/3.6). On `VM_PAGEFAULT`: read `(far, flags)` from message, allocate a frame, `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_PT_MAP)`, `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_CLEAR_PAGEFAULT)`, loop. Uses `minix-ipc` for IPC; no `alloc` crate dependency.
- New `kernel/src/boot_image/elf.rs`: minimal aarch64 ELF64 loader sufficient for VM's binary — iterate PHDRs, for each `PT_LOAD` segment allocate frames, copy file bytes via HHDM, zero BSS tail, map into a new `AddrSpace` with prot derived from `p_flags`. No dynamic relocs.
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs`: replace one of stubs A/B/C *no* — keep A/B/C. Add a new `vm_bootstrap` path that loads the embedded VM ELF (packed into `.boot_image` via the existing infra), creates VM's AS, allocates a stack frame, sets entry/sp, installs priv slot allowing `RECEIVE ANY` + `SYS_VMCTL` + `SEND` to any proc (for replies). VM gets a known endpoint constant.
- New `kernel-shared/src/com.rs` entry: `VM_PROC_NR`, `VM_ENDPOINT = boot_endpoint(VM)`.
- **Page-fault → VM IPC**: page-fault handler now sends `VM_PAGEFAULT` to `VM_ENDPOINT` on the faulting proc's behalf. New `mini_pf_send` (or extend `mini_notify` path) — kernel-originated SEND that queues `deliver_msg` on VM if VM is `RTS_RECEIVING` from ANY, else marks pending on VM's priv slot. The faulting proc stays blocked on `RTS_PAGEFAULT` until `VMCTL_CLEAR_PAGEFAULT` lands. Spell this out in the design comment.
- **Cross-AS IPC user-copy**: replace `read_volatile(deliver_msg_vir)` in `ipc/message.rs` with `walk_pt(target_proc, deliver_msg_vir).ok_or(EFAULT)? + HHDM access`. Now the kernel can deliver a message into a process whose TTBR0 is not currently installed.
- Drop the kernel-resolved heap-window fallback from 3.2; all faults now go to VM. Stub D switches back to "fault on access" (no pre-emptive `VMCTL_PT_MAP`); VM resolves.
- Drop the `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE2` alias added in 3.3.
- `kernel/build.rs` / boot-image packer: ensure the VM ELF is built before the kernel and embedded into `.boot_image`. Use existing `tools/mkbootimage` infra if present; otherwise extend it.
- **Files**: new `servers/vm/src/main.rs`, new `kernel/src/boot_image/elf.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/com.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs` (route pf to VM), `kernel/src/ipc/message.rs` (HHDM walk), possibly new `kernel/src/ipc/pf_send.rs`.
- **Verification**: Boot trace shows VM proc starting, receiving its first `VM_PAGEFAULT`, calling `SYS_VMCTL(PT_MAP)` + `SYS_VMCTL(CLEAR_PAGEFAULT)`. Stub D's faults loop indefinitely with each one round-tripping kernel→VM→kernel. A/B/C ping-pong + SYS_GETINFO still pass. **Phase 3 milestone reached.**

### Slice 3.5 — VM region tracking + VM_BRK

- VM gets a static per-proc region table: `static REGIONS: [[Region; 16]; NR_PROCS]` (or wrapped in an UnsafeCell newtype matching the kernel's PROC_TABLE/PRIV_TABLE convention). Each `Region { vaddr_start, vaddr_end, prot, kind: Kind::{Heap, Mmap, Text, Data, Stack} }`.
- VM populates each proc's regions on first contact (lazy init at first `VM_PAGEFAULT` for that proc): synthesizes Text/Stack/Heap regions from the proc's boot layout (info read via a new `VM_QUERY_BOOT_LAYOUT` `SYS_GETINFO` subcall, or hardcoded constants matching `userland.rs` for now — keep the indirection minimal).
- Page-fault handler in VM: look up `far` in the proc's region table. Heap region → allocate zero frame, install, clear fault. Outside any region → reply with a "VM_FAULT_SIGSEGV" notice (no PM yet, so log + halt the proc).
- New `VM_BRK(new_break)` IPC message handled by VM: extend the heap region's `vaddr_end` up to `new_break` (no eager mapping — pages fault in on first touch).
- Stub D switched to issue `VM_BRK` then touch new bytes.
- **Files**: `servers/vm/src/main.rs`, new `servers/vm/src/region.rs`, new `servers/vm/src/fault.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs` (add `VM_BRK`).
- **Verification**: Trace shows stub D's `VM_BRK` growing the heap region from `0x0100_4000 → 0x0100_8000`; subsequent writes inside the new range succeed, writes outside log the SIGSEGV path.

### Slice 3.6 — VM mmap + Phase 3 cleanup

- `VM_MMAP(addr_hint, length, prot, flags)` and `VM_MUNMAP(addr, length)` IPC messages — anonymous mappings only (file-backed is Phase 5 with VFS). Place at a hint or scan the gap between `[MMAP_BASE = 0x4000_0000, STACK_BASE)` for free space; allocate a region; faults populate frames lazily.
- Stub D mmaps a region and writes to it.
- `SYS_VMCTL` subcall coverage assertion: `const _: () = assert!(NR_VMCTL_SUBCALLS == 6);` (or whatever the final count is) next to the dispatch match in `system/do_vmctl.rs`.
- Stubs A/B/C documented as regression coverage in `docs/plan.md` Phase 3 entry; no deletion.
- `docs/plan.md`: flip each Phase 3 slice marker (`◀ next` / `◀ ready` / `✓ shipped`) inline with PR merges, then write a Phase-3-complete aggregate paragraph at the bottom mirroring the Phase 2.5/2.6 prose style.
- `docs/syscalls.md`: enumerate the new VMCTL subcalls and VM_* call numbers.
- `docs/memory-layout.md`: tighten the per-process TTBR0 + ASID section if anything diverged from what's in the doc today.
- `CLAUDE.md`: add any Phase 3 conventions worth pinning — at minimum the ASID allocation invariant, the "never write TTBR1" rule, and the "frame allocator must not run in pf context" rule.
- **Files**: `servers/vm/src/main.rs`, `servers/vm/src/region.rs`, `servers/vm/src/fault.rs`, new `servers/vm/src/mmap.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`, `docs/plan.md`, `docs/syscalls.md`, `docs/memory-layout.md`, `CLAUDE.md`.
- **Verification**: Stub D mmaps a 4-page region, writes to each page, faults trigger VM, frames allocated, writes succeed. End-of-phase trace shows: A/B ping-pong, C SYS_GETINFO, D VM_BRK + VM_MMAP + on-demand paging — all interleaved with clock ticks, no panics, over an 8s run.

---

## Critical files & reuse

**Existing code to reuse (don't reinvent):**
- `kernel/src/proc/sched.rs` `rts_set` / `rts_unset` — slice 2.5's NLL-safe RTS↔run-queue helpers. Use these for `RTS_PAGEFAULT` in 3.2.
- `kernel/src/proc/sched.rs` `schedule_next` — already flushes `MF_DELIVERMSG` on every EL1→EL0 transition. The cross-AS IPC redesign in 3.4 changes the body of the flush (HHDM walk instead of in-context volatile write), not the call sites.
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/mmu.rs` MAIR_EL1 / TCR_EL1 invariants and PTE bit constants (`PTE_VALID`, `PTE_AF`, `PTE_PXN`, `PTE_UXN`, `PTE_AP_*`, `PTE_SH_INNER`). The new `addrspace.rs` should import these, not re-derive them.
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs::kernel_va_to_pa` — needed by the frame allocator's reserved-range setup in 3.1a and by HHDM access throughout.
- `kernel/src/system/mod.rs` `kernel_call_dispatch` — wrap the new `do_vmctl` into the existing dispatch table, keeping the `k_call_mask` gating and the `assert!(NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3 == N)` compile-time arm check.
- `kernel-shared/src/com.rs` `boot_endpoint(SYSTEM)` — add `VM_PROC_NR` + `boot_endpoint(VM)` alongside.
- `kernel/src/ipc/{send,receive}.rs` — the `mini_pf_send` in 3.4 is a small variant of `mini_send` that runs from kernel context (no caller proc) and targets VM; reuse the same RTS_RECEIVING-check / deliver_msg-queue pattern.

**Files modified per slice** (summary, not exhaustive):
- 3.1a: new `kernel/src/mm/{mod,frame}.rs`, new `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/addrspace.rs`, `kernel/src/main.rs` (smoke test).
- 3.1b: `kernel/src/proc/proc_struct.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs` (rewrite), `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/{trap.S,exception.rs}`, new `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/asid.rs`.
- 3.2: `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs`, new `kernel/src/proc/page_fault.rs`, `kernel/src/proc/{proc_struct,flags}.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/{userland.rs,user_stub.S}`.
- 3.3: new `kernel/src/system/do_vmctl.rs`, `kernel/src/system/mod.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/{userland.rs,user_stub.S}`.
- 3.4: new `servers/vm/src/main.rs`, new `kernel/src/boot_image/elf.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/{userland.rs,exception.rs}`, `kernel/src/ipc/message.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/com.rs`, possibly `tools/mkbootimage`.
- 3.5: `servers/vm/src/main.rs`, new `servers/vm/src/{region,fault}.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`.
- 3.6: `servers/vm/src/{main,region,fault}.rs`, new `servers/vm/src/mmap.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`, `docs/{plan,syscalls,memory-layout}.md`, `CLAUDE.md`.

---

## Verification (per slice — same QEMU rhythm as Phase 2)

```sh
# Build
cargo kernel-aarch64

# Boot. Kernel runs indefinitely once EL0 starts; timeout is mandatory.
# Redirect to a file when grepping — live tail loses lines.
timeout 8 cargo run -p minix4-kernel --target aarch64-unknown-none --release > /tmp/phase3.log 2>&1
```

Each slice's milestone is a grep pattern in `/tmp/phase3.log`:
- **3.1a**: `[mm] frame_alloc OK / map OK / walk OK / free OK`
- **3.1b**: per-proc `TTBR0_EL1 = 0x...... asid=N` lines for procs A/B/C distinct; ping-pong `do_ipc[N]: call_nr=3 ... result=0` trace from slice 2.5 still flowing
- **3.2**: `[pf] proc=D far=0x01000000 → alloc frame=0x........ map retry` then stub D loop
- **3.3**: `[ksys VMCTL_PT_MAP] proc=D va=0x01000000 pa=0x........ result=0`
- **3.4**: VM proc startup line + `[vm] VM_PAGEFAULT from D far=0x... → PT_MAP + CLEAR_PAGEFAULT` round-trip
- **3.5**: VM `[vm] VM_BRK proc=D new_break=0x01008000` then stub D writes succeed
- **3.6**: VM `[vm] VM_MMAP proc=D addr=0x40000000 len=0x4000` then stub D writes succeed across all 4 pages

Always verify the **regressions from earlier phases** still pass on every slice: A/B ping-pong, C SYS_GETINFO, clock-tick interleaving from slice 2.4. Phase 3 ships when the 8s `/tmp/phase3.log` shows all six families of traces with no panic and no `el0_sync_unexpected`.

Host-side unit tests in `kernel-shared` should grow alongside any new types (VMCTL subcall constants, VM_* call numbers, ASID type). `cargo test -p kernel-shared` should stay green every slice.

input.content:
# Phase 3: VM Server + Memory Management

## Context

Phase 2 (slices 2.1–2.6, all shipped) gave MINIX 4 a working microkernel: IPC primitives, GICv3+timer scheduling, SVC kernel-call dispatch with a real `SYS_GETINFO`. Today every EL0 process (stubs A, B, C) shares a **single TTBR0**, all page tables are static `.bss` arrays, there is **no physical frame allocator**, and any EL0 translation fault hits `el0_sync_unexpected` and panics. The `servers/vm` crate is an empty stub; `SYS_VMCTL` is one of the 13 ENOSYS placeholders from slice 2.6.

Phase 3 delivers the milestone **"Boot processes each have isolated address spaces; VM handles page faults"** — per-process page tables with ARMv8 ASIDs, an EL1 page-fault handler, a real user-space VM server with brk + mmap, and the SYS_VMCTL contract that lets VM mediate paging while the kernel retains all unsafe PTE writes. The POSIX fork and exec calls are deferred to Phase 4 (they require the PM server).

**Architecture choices:**
- **PTE ownership**: Kernel writes user PTEs. VM passes `(vaddr, phys_frame, prot, asid)` to `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_PT_MAP)`; the kernel walks the target proc's page table via HHDM and installs the PTE. Keeps all unsafe paging code in the kernel and avoids VM's self-mapping bootstrap problem.
- **ASIDs**: 8-bit ASIDs in `TTBR0_EL1[55:48]` (TCR_EL1.AS left at 0, the Limine default). Allocator panics on overflow; real rollover deferred to Phase 4 when fork actually churns ASIDs.
- **Page-table ownership split**: Kernel owns TTBR1 (untouched from Limine). Each proc gets its own TTBR0 root; context switch updates TTBR0_EL1 with ASID-tagged value and ISBs. TLBI is ASID-tagged, never full-flush.
- **No allocator in VM**: Region tables are static `[Region; N]` arrays per proc inside VM. `linked_list_allocator` deferred until Phase 4 where PM needs it.
- **Kernel reads user memory via HHDM after walking the target proc's page table**, not via `AT S1E0R`. Composes with existing `kernel_va_to_pa` infra and surfaces "VA unmapped" as a clean `Err(EFAULT)`.
- **Stubs A/B/C from Phase 2.5/2.6 stay** as regression coverage; they get migrated to per-proc TTBR0s in 3.1b and run through the rest of Phase 3.

---

## Slice plan (7 PR-sized slices)

Each slice independently builds, boots in QEMU `aarch64-unknown-none`, and prints observable progress. Same cadence as Phase 2. Update `docs/plan.md` slice-status markers (`◀ next` / `◀ ready` / `✓ shipped`) as part of each PR.

### Slice 3.1a — Physical frame allocator + addrspace API (kernel-only, no EL0 changes)

- New `kernel/src/mm/mod.rs` + `mm/frame.rs`: bitmap frame allocator over Limine memmap usable regions. Reserve frames covering: kernel image (`_kern_start` … `_kern_end`), the embedded `.boot_image` section, the existing static stub code/stack pages from Phase 2.5/2.6 (mark all as in-use at boot so they aren't double-allocated). `alloc_frame -> Option<PhysFrame>`, `free_frame(PhysFrame)`, `reserved_range(start, end)`.
- New `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/addrspace.rs`: `AddrSpace::new -> Result<AddrSpace>` allocates an L0 frame and zeros it via HHDM. `map_page(va, pa, prot)` walks/allocates intermediate L1/L2/L3 tables on demand using `alloc_frame`, writes the leaf PTE. `walk_pt(va) -> Option<PhysAddr>` for kernel-side reads later. `destroy` walks all live mappings, frees intermediate tables (freeing leaf frames is the caller's job — leaves room for shared/CoW pages later).
- `kmain` smoke test: after `proc::init`, build a throwaway addrspace, map 4 distinct pages, walk each, destroy it. Trace `[mm] frame_alloc OK / map OK / walk OK / free OK`.
- No `Proc` field changes yet, no userland changes, no IPC changes.
- **Files**: new `kernel/src/mm/{mod,frame}.rs`, new `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/addrspace.rs`, additions to `kernel/src/main.rs` (smoke test gated on a `cfg(feature = "mm_smoke")` or just inlined for the slice and removed in 3.1b).
- **Verification**: QEMU trace shows the smoke-test sequence and clean boot; A/B/C ping-pong + stub C kernel-call unchanged.

### Slice 3.1b — Per-process TTBR0s + ASIDs + minimal pf-diagnostic handler

- Add `Proc::ttbr0_pa: PhysAddr` and `Proc::asid: u8`. Add ASID allocator (8-bit, panic on overflow, single-threaded boot invariant documented).
- Rewrite `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs`: each stub gets its own `AddrSpace`, its own freshly allocated code+stack frames, freshly mapped. Static `L0_TABLE`/`L1_TABLE`/`L2_TABLE`/`L3_CODE_TABLE`/`L3_STACK_TABLE` arrays are removed.
- Context switch (`el1_return_to_user` tail in `trap.S` / `sched::schedule_next` in `proc/sched.rs`): before ERET, write `(ttbr0_pa | (asid as u64) << 48)` into `TTBR0_EL1`, ISB. ASID-tagged TLBI on first switch into a freshly created addrspace. Document and assert that `TTBR1_EL1` is never written (Limine owns it).
- Replace `el0_sync_unexpected` panic for EC=0x20 (instr abort lower EL) and EC=0x24 (data abort lower EL) with a one-page `do_page_fault_stub` that decodes ESR_EL1/FAR_EL1/ELR_EL1 + the faulting proc's name and panics with a clean dump. The real handler lands in 3.2; this slice just trades a generic panic for a useful diagnostic so 3.1b debugging is tractable.
- IPC user-copy in `ipc/message.rs` keeps `read_volatile(deliver_msg_vir)` unchanged — the SVC handler runs in caller context with caller's TTBR0 live, so single-AS reads still work. Cross-AS redesign lands in 3.4 when VM exists.
- **Files**: `kernel/src/proc/proc_struct.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs` (rewrite), `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/trap.S`, new `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/asid.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs`.
- **Verification**: One-shot debug dump at first schedule shows distinct `TTBR0_EL1`/ASID per proc (A=asid1, B=asid2, C=asid3). Existing ping-pong + SYS_GETINFO traces unchanged. Inject a deliberate unmapped EL0 access (temporary debug stub) and see the new diagnostic instead of the generic `el0_sync_unexpected`; remove the deliberate fault before merging.

### Slice 3.2 — Real page-fault handler + RTS_PAGEFAULT (kernel-resolved)

- Promote the 3.1b diagnostic into `do_page_fault(esr, far, elr)`: classify (translation vs permission), set caller's `RTS_PAGEFAULT`, record `(addr, flags, ip)` in a new `Proc::page_fault_state` field, dequeue from runqueue using the slice-2.5 `rts_set` helper (capture-then-borrow-end NLL pattern), call `reschedule`.
- For this slice, the kernel resolves itself: each proc has a "heap window" range stored in `Proc` (set by userland.rs at boot — e.g., `[0x0100_0000, 0x0100_4000)`). If `far` falls in that window, kernel allocates a frame via 3.1a's allocator, maps it RW+NX via the proc's addrspace, ASID-tagged TLBI, clears `RTS_PAGEFAULT` via `rts_unset`, requeues. Otherwise hand off to the panic path (real SIGSEGV is Phase 4 PM work).
- Page-fault flow runs **outside** allocator-from-fault-context concern: the handler enqueues and returns to the scheduler before any allocation; allocation happens when the proc (or VM in later slices) is next scheduled. Document this invariant.
- Add a 4th EL0 stub D (ProcNr 14, Priv slot 19, code at 0x40_0000-aligned VA distinct from A/B/C, heap window 0x0100_0000+): touches an unmapped page in its heap window, loops. Stub D shares one IMAGE entry like C — minimal extra `install_stub_d_priv` helper.
- **Files**: `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs`, new `kernel/src/proc/page_fault.rs`, `kernel/src/proc/proc_struct.rs` (add `page_fault_state` + `heap_window`), `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs` (stub D + heap window setup), `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/user_stub.S` (stub D blob), new RTS flag in `kernel/src/proc/flags.rs`.
- **Verification**: QEMU trace `[pf] proc=D far=0x01000000 → alloc frame=0xXXX, map, retry`; stub D loops without panic. A/B/C unchanged.

### Slice 3.3 — Real SYS_VMCTL subcalls, exercised from a stub (no VM yet)

- New `kernel/src/system/do_vmctl.rs` replacing the `stubs::do_vmctl` ENOSYS placeholder. Subcalls:
  - `VMCTL_PT_MAP(endpoint, vaddr, pa, prot)` — install one PTE in target proc's AS via the 3.1a `map_page` path. Returns OK or EINVAL/ENOMEM.
  - `VMCTL_PT_UNMAP(endpoint, vaddr)` — clear one PTE + ASID-tagged TLBI.
  - `VMCTL_CLEAR_PAGEFAULT(endpoint)` — `rts_unset(RTS_PAGEFAULT)` on target, requeue.
  - `VMCTL_GET_PAGEFAULT(endpoint, &out)` — return `page_fault_state` to caller (only valid while target has `RTS_PAGEFAULT` set).
  - `VMCTL_VMINHIBIT_SET / _CLEAR(endpoint)` — block scheduling of target while VM mutates its AS.
- `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`: add `VMCTL_*` subcall constants. Add a new `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3 = 14` (calls list unchanged, just renamed for the next slice's const-assert; keep `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE2` as an alias for one slice to minimize churn — drop in 3.4). The compile-time `assert!` in `system/mod.rs` updates to the new name.
- Switch the 3.2 kernel-resolved heap-window into stub D *itself* calling `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_PT_MAP)` to install heap pages it pre-faulted on. This exercises the full SYS_VMCTL surface end-to-end without inventing a "fake VM in SYSTEM". Stub D's priv slot gains `SYS_VMCTL` in its `k_call_mask`.
- The kernel-resolved fast path from 3.2 stays in place but is now reachable only from procs *without* `SYS_VMCTL` access — keeps the slice composable.
- **Files**: new `kernel/src/system/do_vmctl.rs`, `kernel/src/system/mod.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/user_stub.S` (stub D rewritten to issue SYS_VMCTL), `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs` (stub D priv update).
- **Verification**: Since stub D can't run while blocked on RTS_PAGEFAULT, this slice's test pattern is **pre-emptive**: stub D first calls `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_PT_MAP)` to populate its heap, then accesses it. The on-demand fault → VMCTL_GET_PAGEFAULT path is exercised by VM in 3.4. Trace shows `[ksys VMCTL_PT_MAP] proc=D va=0x01000000 pa=0xXXX result=0` then successful access.

### Slice 3.4 — Real VM server boots, ELF loader, cross-AS IPC delivery

- `servers/vm/src/main.rs`: minimal sef-style receive loop. Handles `VM_PAGEFAULT` only (mmap/brk in 3.5/3.6). On `VM_PAGEFAULT`: read `(far, flags)` from message, allocate a frame, `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_PT_MAP)`, `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_CLEAR_PAGEFAULT)`, loop. Uses `minix-ipc` for IPC; no `alloc` crate dependency.
- New `kernel/src/boot_image/elf.rs`: minimal aarch64 ELF64 loader sufficient for VM's binary — iterate PHDRs, for each `PT_LOAD` segment allocate frames, copy file bytes via HHDM, zero BSS tail, map into a new `AddrSpace` with prot derived from `p_flags`. No dynamic relocs.
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs`: keep A/B/C. Add a new `vm_bootstrap` path that loads the embedded VM ELF (packed into `.boot_image` via the existing infra), creates VM's AS, allocates a stack frame, sets entry/sp, installs priv slot allowing `RECEIVE ANY` + `SYS_VMCTL` + `SEND` to any proc (for replies). VM gets a known endpoint constant.
- New `kernel-shared/src/com.rs` entry: `VM_PROC_NR`, `VM_ENDPOINT = boot_endpoint(VM)`.
- **Page-fault → VM IPC**: page-fault handler now sends `VM_PAGEFAULT` to `VM_ENDPOINT` on the faulting proc's behalf. New `mini_pf_send` (or extend `mini_notify` path) — kernel-originated SEND that queues `deliver_msg` on VM if VM is `RTS_RECEIVING` from ANY, else marks pending on VM's priv slot. The faulting proc stays blocked on `RTS_PAGEFAULT` until `VMCTL_CLEAR_PAGEFAULT` lands. Spell this out in the design comment.
- **Cross-AS IPC user-copy**: replace `read_volatile(deliver_msg_vir)` in `ipc/message.rs` with `walk_pt(target_proc, deliver_msg_vir).ok_or(EFAULT)? + HHDM access`. Now the kernel can deliver a message into a process whose TTBR0 is not currently installed.
- Drop the kernel-resolved heap-window fallback from 3.2; all faults now go to VM. Stub D switches back to "fault on access" (no pre-emptive `VMCTL_PT_MAP`); VM resolves.
- Drop the `NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE2` alias added in 3.3.
- `kernel/build.rs` / boot-image packer: ensure the VM ELF is built before the kernel and embedded into `.boot_image`. Use existing `tools/mkbootimage` infra if present; otherwise extend it.
- **Files**: new `servers/vm/src/main.rs`, new `kernel/src/boot_image/elf.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/com.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs` (route pf to VM), `kernel/src/ipc/message.rs` (HHDM walk), possibly new `kernel/src/ipc/pf_send.rs`.
- **Verification**: Boot trace shows VM proc starting, receiving its first `VM_PAGEFAULT`, calling `SYS_VMCTL(PT_MAP)` + `SYS_VMCTL(CLEAR_PAGEFAULT)`. Stub D's faults loop indefinitely with each one round-tripping kernel→VM→kernel. A/B/C ping-pong + SYS_GETINFO still pass. **Phase 3 milestone reached.**

### Slice 3.5 — VM region tracking + VM_BRK

- VM gets a static per-proc region table: `static REGIONS: [[Region; 16]; NR_PROCS]` (or wrapped in an UnsafeCell newtype matching the kernel's PROC_TABLE/PRIV_TABLE convention). Each `Region { vaddr_start, vaddr_end, prot, kind: Kind::{Heap, Mmap, Text, Data, Stack} }`.
- VM populates each proc's regions on first contact (lazy init at first `VM_PAGEFAULT` for that proc): synthesizes Text/Stack/Heap regions from the proc's boot layout (info read via a new `VM_QUERY_BOOT_LAYOUT` `SYS_GETINFO` subcall, or hardcoded constants matching `userland.rs` for now — keep the indirection minimal).
- Page-fault handler in VM: look up `far` in the proc's region table. Heap region → allocate zero frame, install, clear fault. Outside any region → reply with a "VM_FAULT_SIGSEGV" notice (no PM yet, so log + halt the proc).
- New `VM_BRK(new_break)` IPC message handled by VM: extend the heap region's `vaddr_end` up to `new_break` (no eager mapping — pages fault in on first touch).
- Stub D switched to issue `VM_BRK` then touch new bytes.
- **Files**: `servers/vm/src/main.rs`, new `servers/vm/src/region.rs`, new `servers/vm/src/fault.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs` (add `VM_BRK`).
- **Verification**: Trace shows stub D's `VM_BRK` growing the heap region from `0x0100_4000 → 0x0100_8000`; subsequent writes inside the new range succeed, writes outside log the SIGSEGV path.

### Slice 3.6 — VM mmap + Phase 3 cleanup

- `VM_MMAP(addr_hint, length, prot, flags)` and `VM_MUNMAP(addr, length)` IPC messages — anonymous mappings only (file-backed is Phase 5 with VFS). Place at a hint or scan the gap between `[MMAP_BASE = 0x4000_0000, STACK_BASE)` for free space; allocate a region; faults populate frames lazily.
- Stub D mmaps a region and writes to it.
- `SYS_VMCTL` subcall coverage assertion: `const _: () = assert!(NR_VMCTL_SUBCALLS == 6);` (or whatever the final count is) next to the dispatch match in `system/do_vmctl.rs`.
- Stubs A/B/C documented as regression coverage in `docs/plan.md` Phase 3 entry; no deletion.
- `docs/plan.md`: flip each Phase 3 slice marker (`◀ next` / `◀ ready` / `✓ shipped`) inline with PR merges, then write a Phase-3-complete aggregate paragraph at the bottom mirroring the Phase 2.5/2.6 prose style.
- `docs/syscalls.md`: enumerate the new VMCTL subcalls and VM_* call numbers.
- `docs/memory-layout.md`: tighten the per-process TTBR0 + ASID section if anything diverged from what's in the doc today.
- `CLAUDE.md`: add any Phase 3 conventions worth pinning — at minimum the ASID allocation invariant, the "never write TTBR1" rule, and the "frame allocator must not run in pf context" rule.
- **Files**: `servers/vm/src/main.rs`, `servers/vm/src/region.rs`, `servers/vm/src/fault.rs`, new `servers/vm/src/mmap.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`, `docs/plan.md`, `docs/syscalls.md`, `docs/memory-layout.md`, `CLAUDE.md`.
- **Verification**: Stub D mmaps a 4-page region, writes to each page, faults trigger VM, frames allocated, writes succeed. End-of-phase trace shows: A/B ping-pong, C SYS_GETINFO, D VM_BRK + VM_MMAP + on-demand paging — all interleaved with clock ticks, no panics, over an 8s run.

---

## Critical files & reuse

**Existing code to reuse (don't reinvent):**
- `kernel/src/proc/sched.rs` `rts_set` / `rts_unset` — slice 2.5's NLL-safe RTS↔run-queue helpers. Use these for `RTS_PAGEFAULT` in 3.2.
- `kernel/src/proc/sched.rs` `schedule_next` — already flushes `MF_DELIVERMSG` on every EL1→EL0 transition. The cross-AS IPC redesign in 3.4 changes the body of the flush (HHDM walk instead of in-context volatile write), not the call sites.
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/mmu.rs` MAIR_EL1 / TCR_EL1 invariants and PTE bit constants (`PTE_VALID`, `PTE_AF`, `PTE_PXN`, `PTE_UXN`, `PTE_AP_*`, `PTE_SH_INNER`). The new `addrspace.rs` should import these, not re-derive them.
- `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs::kernel_va_to_pa` — needed by the frame allocator's reserved-range setup in 3.1a and by HHDM access throughout.
- `kernel/src/system/mod.rs` `kernel_call_dispatch` — wrap the new `do_vmctl` into the existing dispatch table, keeping the `k_call_mask` gating and the `assert!(NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3 == N)` compile-time arm check.
- `kernel-shared/src/com.rs` `boot_endpoint(SYSTEM)` — add `VM_PROC_NR` + `boot_endpoint(VM)` alongside.
- `kernel/src/ipc/{send,receive}.rs` — the `mini_pf_send` in 3.4 is a small variant of `mini_send` that runs from kernel context (no caller proc) and targets VM; reuse the same RTS_RECEIVING-check / deliver_msg-queue pattern.

**Files modified per slice** (summary, not exhaustive):
- 3.1a: new `kernel/src/mm/{mod,frame}.rs`, new `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/addrspace.rs`, `kernel/src/main.rs` (smoke test).
- 3.1b: `kernel/src/proc/proc_struct.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs` (rewrite), `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/{trap.S,exception.rs}`, new `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/asid.rs`.
- 3.2: `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs`, new `kernel/src/proc/page_fault.rs`, `kernel/src/proc/{proc_struct,flags}.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/{userland.rs,user_stub.S}`.
- 3.3: new `kernel/src/system/do_vmctl.rs`, `kernel/src/system/mod.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/{userland.rs,user_stub.S}`.
- 3.4: new `servers/vm/src/main.rs`, new `kernel/src/boot_image/elf.rs`, `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/{userland.rs,exception.rs}`, `kernel/src/ipc/message.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/com.rs`, possibly `tools/mkbootimage`.
- 3.5: `servers/vm/src/main.rs`, new `servers/vm/src/{region,fault}.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`.
- 3.6: `servers/vm/src/{main,region,fault}.rs`, new `servers/vm/src/mmap.rs`, `kernel-shared/src/callnr.rs`, `docs/{plan,syscalls,memory-layout}.md`, `CLAUDE.md`.

---

## Verification (per slice — same QEMU rhythm as Phase 2)

```sh
# Build
cargo kernel-aarch64

# Boot. Kernel runs indefinitely once EL0 starts; timeout is mandatory.
# Redirect to a file when grepping — live tail loses lines.
timeout 8 cargo run -p minix4-kernel --target aarch64-unknown-none --release > /tmp/phase3.log 2>&1
```

Each slice's milestone is a grep pattern in `/tmp/phase3.log`:
- **3.1a**: `[mm] frame_alloc OK / map OK / walk OK / free OK`
- **3.1b**: per-proc `TTBR0_EL1 = 0x...... asid=N` lines for procs A/B/C distinct; ping-pong `do_ipc[N]: call_nr=3 ... result=0` trace from slice 2.5 still flowing
- **3.2**: `[pf] proc=D far=0x01000000 → alloc frame=0x........ map retry` then stub D loop
- **3.3**: `[ksys VMCTL_PT_MAP] proc=D va=0x01000000 pa=0x........ result=0`
- **3.4**: VM proc startup line + `[vm] VM_PAGEFAULT from D far=0x... → PT_MAP + CLEAR_PAGEFAULT` round-trip
- **3.5**: VM `[vm] VM_BRK proc=D new_break=0x01008000` then stub D writes succeed
- **3.6**: VM `[vm] VM_MMAP proc=D addr=0x40000000 len=0x4000` then stub D writes succeed across all 4 pages

Always verify the **regressions from earlier phases** still pass on every slice: A/B ping-pong, C SYS_GETINFO, clock-tick interleaving from slice 2.4. Phase 3 ships when the 8s `/tmp/phase3.log` shows all six families of traces with no panic and no `el0_sync_unexpected`.

Host-side unit tests in `kernel-shared` should grow alongside any new types (VMCTL subcall constants, VM_* call numbers, ASID type). `cargo test -p kernel-shared` should stay green every slice.
